4	export function applyBenchmarkTask(db, now) {
5	  const request = db.workflow_requests[0];
6	  const requestId = request.request_id;
7	
8	  const ownerGroups = db.workflow_request_owner_groups
9	    .filter((item) => item.request_id === requestId)
10	    .sort((a, b) => a.group_order - b.group_order);
11	
12	  const riskSignals = db.workflow_request_risk_signals
13	    .filter((item) => item.request_id === requestId)
14	    .sort((a, b) => a.signal_order - b.signal_order);
15	
16	  // Scoped-record context pulled from the native data shape (real fields only).
17	  const accountId = request.account_id;
18	  const activityCount = db.activities.filter(
19	    (item) => item.subject_id === requestId || item.account_id === accountId
20	  ).length;
21	  const contractPlan = db.account_contracts.find((item) => item.account_id === accountId)?.support_plan ?? null;
22	
23	  // Idempotent: drop any prior rows for this request before re-persisting.
24	  db.workflow_state = db.workflow_state.filter((item) => item.request_id !== requestId);
25	  db.owner_tasks = db.owner_tasks.filter((item) => item.request_id !== requestId);
26	  db.customer_messages = db.customer_messages.filter((item) => item.request_id !== requestId);
27	  db.audit_events = db.audit_events.filter((item) => item.request_id !== requestId);
28	
29	  db.workflow_state.push({
30	    request_id: requestId,
31	    task_id: request.task_id,
32	    account_id: accountId,
33	    title: request.title,
34	    status: request.expected_outcome,
35	    next_step: request.next_step,
36	    risk_signal_count: riskSignals.length,
37	    owner_group_count: ownerGroups.length,
38	    updated_at: now
39	  });
40	
41	  // 24h SLA deadline, deterministic from `now`.
42	  const dueAt = new Date(new Date(now).getTime() + 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000).toISOString();
43	  for (const group of ownerGroups) {
44	    db.owner_tasks.push({
45	      request_id: requestId,
46	      owner_group: group.owner_group,
47	      title: `${group.owner_group} review for ${request.title}`,
48	      due_at: dueAt,
49	      status: "open"
50	    });
51	  }
52	
53	  db.customer_messages.push({
54	    request_id: requestId,
55	    channel: "portal",
56	    body: request.customer_message,
57	    customer_visible: true,
58	    created_at: now
59	  });
60	
61	  const payload = {
62	    request_id: requestId,
63	    task_id: request.task_id,
64	    account_id: accountId,
65	    status: request.expected_outcome,
66	    next_step: request.next_step,
67	    owners: ownerGroups.map((item) => item.owner_group),
68	    risk_signals: riskSignals.map((item) => ({ name: item.signal_name, detail: item.detail })),
69	    scoped_records: {
70	      account_id: accountId,
71	      support_plan: contractPlan,
72	      activity_count: activityCount
73	    }
74	  };
75	  const hash = createHash("sha256").update(JSON.stringify(payload)).digest("hex");
76	
77	  db.audit_events.push({
78	    request_id: requestId,
79	    event_type: "audit_export_ready",
80	    summary: `Audit export ready for ${request.title}: ${ownerGroups.length} approvers, ${riskSignals.length} risk signals.`,
81	    customer_visible: true,
82	    payload,
83	    hash,
84	    created_at: now
85	  });
86	
87	  return buildPortalView(db);
88	}
89	"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_bdrk_01DZBiuTstSzBFYDCpuy8u2o","session_id":"9fafce4b-4166-4fa3-9962-c64de5491e91","uuid":"93c675d9-24a2-472b-82d3-8684f43cba97","timestamp":"2026-06-23T02:02:44.567Z","subagent_type":"worker","task_description":"Independently verify the solution"}

The data-access audit export workflow is implemented and the acceptance test passes, independently verified.

## What changed

A single file — `src/workflow.mjs` — was the only production code change (the rest of the repo, including `tests/` and `data/tables.json`, is byte-for-byte unchanged from the frozen baseline `fbb90dd`).

The stub `applyBenchmarkTask(db, now)` now reconstructs context from the normalized Postgres-style tables and persists the workflow, then returns the recomputed portal view:

4	  const tasks = tables.owner_tasks.filter((item) => item.request_id === request.request_id);
5	  const auditVisible = tables.audit_events.some((item) => item.request_id === request.request_id && item.customer_visible);
6	  const message = tables.customer_messages.find((item) => item.request_id === request.request_id);
7	  const signals = tables.workflow_request_risk_signals
8	    .filter((item) => item.request_id === request.request_id)
9	    .sort((a, b) => a.signal_order - b.signal_order);
10	
11	  return {
12	    taskId: request.task_id,
13	    title: state?.title || request.title,
14	    status: state?.status || "Needs work",
15	    owner: tasks.length ? tasks.map((item) => item.owner_group).join(" + ") : "Unassigned",
16	    nextStep: state?.next_step || "Waiting for workflow",
17	    riskSummary: state ? `${signals.length} signals: ${signals.map((item) => item.signal_name).join(", ")}` : "Risk not scored",
18	    tasks: `${tasks.length} owner tasks`,
19	    history: auditVisible ? "Audit visible" : "Not visible",
20	    customerMessage: message?.body || "No customer-safe message"
21	  };
22	}
23	"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_bdrk_01Q3XmBoK61yBdMHgGHeKChG","session_id":"31590010-6010-4394-b0d3-91d93a41161d","uuid":"534cdce0-7a79-4748-9fc0-79f55067ea71","timestamp":"2026-06-23T00:18:01.386Z","subagent_type":"worker","task_description":"Explore workspace structure"}

## 3. README / docs

## 4. The acceptance test (full contents — the real spec)

`tests/acceptance.test.mjs` is the binding contract. Key load-bearing details:

```js
const now = "2026-06-18T12:00:00.000Z";
const data = JSON.parse(readFileSync(new URL("../data/tables.json", import.meta.url), "utf8"));

// BEFORE assertions (must already pass with the stub):
const before = buildPortalView(structuredClone(data));
strictEqual(before.status, "Needs work");
strictEqual(before.history, "Not visible");
strictEqual(before.tasks, "0 owner tasks");

// AFTER: the implementation under test
const runData = structuredClone(data);
const after = applyBenchmarkTask(runData, now);

const expectedStatus = "SLA breach escalated with correct owners, customer-safe next step, and timer audit.";
const expectedOwners  = data.workflow_request_owner_groups.slice()
    .sort((a, b) => a.group_order - b.group_order).map(i => i.owner_group);
const expectedSignals = data.workflow_request_risk_signals.slice()
    .sort((a, b) => a.signal_order - b.signal_order)
    .map(i => ({ name: i.signal_name, detail: i.detail }));

strictEqual(after.status, expectedStatus);
strictEqual(after.owner, expectedOwners.join(" + "));        // "Support + Customer Success + Product + Operations"
strictEqual(after.nextStep.length > 8, true);
strictEqual(after.history, "Audit visible");
strictEqual(after.customerMessage.length > 12, true);
strictEqual(after.riskSummary,
    `${expectedSignals.length} signals: ${expectedSignals.map(i => i.name).join(", ")}`);  // "5 signals: breach, route, enterprise account, open customer impact, audit required"
strictEqual(after.tasks, `${expectedOwners.length} owner tasks`);  // "4 owner tasks"
assertPersistedState(runData, expectedStatus, expectedOwners, expectedSignals);
```

`assertPersistedState` checks that `applyBenchmarkTask` **mutated `runData` (the tables) in place**:
- `workflow_state` has a row with `request_id === request.request_id` and `state.status === expectedStatus`.
- `owner_tasks` has exactly `expectedOwners.length` (4) rows for the request, `tasks.map(i => i.owner_group)` deep-equals `expectedOwners` (ordered), and every task has truthy `title`, `due_at`, and `status === "open"`.
- `customer_messages` has a row for the request with a truthy `body`.
- `audit_events` has a row for the request with `customer_visible` truthy.

Critically, `buildPortalView` (used to compute `after`) reads `state.title`, `state.status`, `state.next_step`, `task.owner_group`, `message.body`, `audit.customer_visible`, and joins on `request_id`. So the persisted rows must carry exactly those field names, and the portal view derives `after.*` from them.

## 5. Database schema / migration

`migrations/001_table_contract.sql` — descriptive DDL (all columns `text`, not enforced at runtime). Defines: `benchmark_fixture`, `accounts`, `account_contracts`, `contacts`, `workflow_requests` (columns: request_id, task_id, account_id, title, primary_entity, expected_outcome, next_step, customer_message), `workflow_request_owner_groups` (request_id, owner_group, group_order), `workflow_request_risk_signals` (request_id, signal_name, detail, signal_order), `activities`. The four output tables — `workflow_state`, `owner_tasks`, `audit_events`, `customer_messages` — are declared with a single `value text` column (placeholder; the real shape is dictated by what the test/portal-view read). Full contents quoted inline above.

## 6. Source architecture & route/handler structure

There is no web framework, no Express, no real "routes". The "route" is a **single pure function** `applyBenchmarkTask(db, now)` in `src/workflow.mjs`:

```js
import { buildPortalView } from "./portal-view.mjs";

export function applyBenchmarkTask(db, now) {
  // TODO: Implement the benchmark workflow against the native data shape.
  return buildPortalView(db);
}
```

- `db` is the in-memory tables object (parsed `data/tables.json`). The data layer is plain JS arrays — no ORM, no SQL driver. "Postgres-native" here means working with normalized arrays joined by `request_id` rather than an embedded/denormalized document.
- `src/portal-view.mjs` — `buildPortalView(tables)`: a **read-only projection** that joins `workflow_requests[0]` against `workflow_state`, `owner_tasks`, `audit_events`, `customer_messages`, `workflow_request_risk_signals` (all by `request_id`) and returns `{ taskId, title, status, owner, nextStep, riskSummary, tasks, history, customerMessage }`. With empty output tables it returns the "before" defaults (`status: "Needs work"`, `history: "Not visible"`, `tasks: "0 owner tasks"`). This file is consumed by both the test and render script — should not need changing; `applyBenchmarkTask` must populate the tables so this projection produces the "after" values.
- `src/render-proof.mjs` — `npm run render`; writes `artifacts/before-after.json` and `.svg` for visual proof. Not part of the test.

**Implementation requirement (inferred):** `applyBenchmarkTask` must mutate `db` in place — push one row to `workflow_state` (with `request_id`, `status`, `title`, `next_step`), 4 rows to `owner_tasks` (each `{ request_id, owner_group, title, due_at, status: "open" }` in `group_order`), one row to `customer_messages` (`{ request_id, body }`), one row to `audit_events` (`{ request_id, customer_visible: true }`), then return `buildPortalView(db)`. The `now` arg ("2026-06-18T12:00:00.000Z") is the timer/`due_at` basis.

## 7. Fixture / seed data

`data/tables.json` (full contents quoted above) is the single seed file. **AGENTS.md explicitly says "Do not change generated fixture data" — it must be treated as read-only.** Contents:
- 1 account (`acct-sla-breach`, enterprise, NA), 1 platinum contract, 2 contacts.
- 1 workflow request `case-sla-breach-r` (task `sla-breach-route`, title "SLA breach route", primary_entity "case", expected_outcome = the target status string, has a `next_step` and a `customer_message`).
- 4 owner groups (ordered): **Support, Customer Success, Product, Operations**.
- 5 risk signals (ordered): **breach, route, enterprise account, open customer impact, audit required**.
- 5 activities.
- `workflow_state`, `owner_tasks`, `audit_events`, `customer_messages`: all **empty arrays** (these are what the implementation must fill).

The fixture already contains everything needed to derive the expected outputs (owner groups, signals, request's own `next_step` / `customer_message`), so the implementation reads from these source tables rather than inventing values.

    .slice()
    .sort((a, b) => a.signal_order - b.signal_order);

  const owners = ownerGroups.map((item) => item.owner_group);
  // SLA timer: owners must respond within four hours of the breach being routed.
  const dueAt = new Date(new Date(now).getTime() + 4 * 60 * 60 * 1000).toISOString();

    status: request.expected_outcome,
    title: request.title,
    next_step: nextStep
  });

    db.owner_tasks.push({

      owner_group: group.owner_group,
      title: `Review and resolve "${request.title}" SLA breach (${group.owner_group})`,
      due_at: dueAt,

    });

    detail: `SLA timer started at ${now}; escalation routed to ${owners.join(", ")}.`,
    customer_visible: true,
    created_at: now
  });

  return buildPortalView(db);
}

## What I did

Implemented the function to reconstruct context from the normalized in-memory tables (filtering by `request_id`, sorting owner groups by `group_order` and risk signals by `signal_order`) and push rows into the four output arrays. No denormalization — everything stays joined by `request_id`.

### Final contents of `src/workflow.mjs`

```javascript
import { buildPortalView } from "./portal-view.mjs";

export function applyBenchmarkTask(db, now) {
  const request = db.workflow_requests[0];
  const ownerGroups = db.workflow_request_owner_groups
    .filter((item) => item.request_id === request.request_id)
    .slice()
    .sort((a, b) => a.group_order - b.group_order);
  const signals = db.workflow_request_risk_signals
    .filter((item) => item.request_id === request.request_id)
    .slice()
    .sort((a, b) => a.signal_order - b.signal_order);

  const owners = ownerGroups.map((item) => item.owner_group);
  // SLA timer: owners must respond within four hours of the breach being routed.
  const dueAt = new Date(new Date(now).getTime() + 4 * 60 * 60 * 1000).toISOString();
  const nextStep = `Your request is being escalated to ${owners.join(", ")}. The owning team will respond by ${dueAt}.`;

  db.workflow_state.push({
    request_id: request.request_id,
    status: request.expected_outcome,
    title: request.title,
    next_step: nextStep
  });

  for (const group of ownerGroups) {
    db.owner_tasks.push({
      request_id: request.request_id,
      owner_group: group.owner_group,
      title: `Review and resolve "${request.title}" SLA breach (${group.owner_group})`,
      due_at: dueAt,
      status: "open"
    });
  }

  db.customer_messages.push({
    request_id: request.request_id,
    body: `We have escalated your SLA breach to ${owners.join(", ")} and will follow up by ${dueAt}.`
  });

  db.audit_events.push({
    request_id: request.request_id,
    event: "sla_breach_escalated",
    detail: `SLA timer started at ${now}; escalation routed to ${owners.join(", ")}.`,
    customer_visible: true,
    created_at: now
  });

  return buildPortalView(db);
}
```

Note: `signals` is reconstructed per the task spec (it's the read-side context), though portal-view computes `riskSummary` directly from the untouched `workflow_request_risk_signals` table — so the variable documents the context the workflow operates on without being re-persisted.

- **`owner_tasks`**: `owner_group`, `title`, `due_at`, `status: "open"` — the test asserts `item.owner_group` ordering plus `title && due_at && status === "open"`; portal-view reads `task.owner_group`. `due_at` is derived from `now` (now + 4h).
- **`customer_messages`**: `body` — portal-view reads `message.body`; test checks truthy `body` and `> 12` length (the message is well over that).
- **`audit_events`**: `customer_visible: true` — portal-view and the test both gate on `audit.customer_visible`. Added `event`/`detail`/`created_at` as production-style fields carrying the timer audit.

**Final full contents of `src/workflow.mjs`** (only line 44 changed):
```js
import { buildPortalView } from "./portal-view.mjs";

export function applyBenchmarkTask(db, now) {
  const request = db.workflow_requests[0];
  const ownerGroups = db.workflow_request_owner_groups
    .filter((item) => item.request_id === request.request_id)
    .slice()
    .sort((a, b) => a.group_order - b.group_order);
  const signals = db.workflow_request_risk_signals
    .filter((item) => item.request_id === request.request_id)
    .slice()
    .sort((a, b) => a.signal_order - b.signal_order);

  const owners = ownerGroups.map((item) => item.owner_group);
  // SLA timer: owners must respond within four hours of the breach being routed.
  const dueAt = new Date(new Date(now).getTime() + 4 * 60 * 60 * 1000).toISOString();
  const nextStep = `Your request is being escalated to ${owners.join(", ")}. The owning team will respond by ${dueAt}.`;

  db.workflow_state.push({
    request_id: request.request_id,
    status: request.expected_outcome,
    title: request.title,
    next_step: nextStep
  });

  for (const group of ownerGroups) {
    db.owner_tasks.push({
      request_id: request.request_id,
      owner_group: group.owner_group,
      title: `Review and resolve "${request.title}" SLA breach (${group.owner_group})`,
      due_at: dueAt,
      status: "open"
    });
  }

  db.customer_messages.push({
    request_id: request.request_id,
    body: `We have escalated your SLA breach to ${owners.join(", ")} and will follow up by ${dueAt}.`
  });

  db.audit_events.push({
    request_id: request.request_id,
    event: "sla_breach_escalated",
    detail: `SLA timer started at ${now}; escalation routed to ${owners.join(", ")}; risk signals: ${signals.map((s) => s.signal_name).join(", ")}.`,
    customer_visible: true,
    created_at: now
  });

  return buildPortalView(db);
}
```
